front: Opera: the oldest opera still regularly performed, whose trumpet toccata opens it and whose protagonist sings 'Rosa del ciel' at his wedding
Opera: Alessandro Striggio wrote its libretto; the original ending had the protagonist chased by the Maenads
Opera: Cardinal Mazarin commissioned it; its epilogue compares the protagonist's fate to France and compares him to the Fleur-de-lis; one of only two operas by Luigi Rossi
Opera: the first of its composer's 'reform operas,' featuring the 'Dance of the Blessed Spirits'
Opera: its composer published a manifesto decrying da capo arias and secco recitatives
Opera: Amore sets conditions for the protagonist's return in 'Si les doux accords,' then prevents his suicide in 'Tendre amour'
Opera: its lead male role was sung by a castrato in Vienna but not in the reworked French premiere
Opera: features the aria 'Che faro senza Euridice,' sung after the protagonist loses his wife a second time
Opera: the Infernal Galop from its second act became synonymous with the can-can at the Folies-Bergère
Opera: the allegorical figure Public Opinion pressures the violin-teacher protagonist to rescue his wife, whom he openly detests
Opera: the protagonist tells his wife he detests her because she invented hexameter
Opera: a jailer sings of his nostalgia for his time as the King of Boeotia
Opera: in its 'Fly Duet,' a character transformed into a fly can only buzz rather than sing during a love duet
Opera: ends when a Jupiter lightning bolt startles the protagonist into looking back at his wife
Composer: parodied the Infernal Galop from Offenbach's Orpheus in the Underworld at a painstakingly slow string tempo to depict tortoises in Carnival of the Animals
Opera: its second act sends the protagonist through seventeen arches from Countryside to Fear; three 'Passing Clouds' narrate the myths of Dionysus, Lycurgus, and Pentheus
Opera: Birtwistle opera with multiple 'hysterical' arias and IRCAM-composed music by Barry Anderson, centered on the protagonist's 'mask'
Film: 1959 Marcel Camus film set during Rio's Carnaval in which the protagonist is a Black trolley conductor said to make the sun rise
Film: Luiz Bonfa's score helped popularize bossa nova internationally; 'Manhã de Carnaval' is known in the US as 'Black Orpheus'
Film: Vince Guaraldi's Jazz Impressions of this film included his breakthrough hit 'Cast Your Fate to the Wind'
Film: adapts Vinicius de Moraes's play Orpheus of the Conception, for which de Moraes and Tom Jobim wrote 'Lamento no Morro'
Opera: Orpheus sings 'Possente spirto' to the ferryman Charon to lull him to sleep
Musical: Anaïs Mitchell folk musical with five named characters (Hermes, Persephone, Hades, Orpheus, Eurydice); Hermes opens with 'Road to Hell'
